contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Use a moving window when one sequential traversal can maintain exactly the state for the current contiguous interval; use prefix state when arbitrary interval values must be reconstructed from precomputed cumulative information.

items.prompt: A single pass must report the maximum sum among all contiguous blocks of exactly k elements. Which state model is most direct?

options.text: Maintain the current k-element sum, subtract the outgoing value, and add the incoming value.
Build a full prefix array and issue one query for every block because precomputation is mandatory.
Recompute each block sum from its k elements.
Sort values before comparing block sums.

feedback.reason: The blocks are visited in adjacent order and differ by one outgoing and one incoming value.
feedback.details: A moving aggregate reuses exactly that overlap and needs only the current sum plus boundaries.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_prefix_queries: Prefix sums are correct, but the full array is not required for this one sequential sweep.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.recompute_each: This repeats overlapping work and can cost O(nk).

items.prompt: An immutable array receives many queries asking for the sum from index l through r. Which representation is most reusable?

options.text: Precompute cumulative sums so each interval is one subtraction.
Keep one current window and move it to every arbitrary query.
Sort the array before answering ranges.
Rescan l through r for every query regardless of query volume.

feedback.reason: Random interval queries need a representation independent of one current boundary pair.
feedback.details: With P[i + 1] = P[i] + a[i], rangeSum(l, r) = P[r + 1] - P[l].

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_moving_window: Arbitrary queries may jump and resize, so one moving state is not naturally reusable.

items.prompt: A fixed-size window moves from [l, r] to [l + 1, r + 1]. Which update preserves its sum?

options.text: Subtract a[l] and add a[r + 1].
Add both boundary values.
Subtract both boundary values.
Subtract the entire old sum from the new sum.

feedback.reason: Adjacent equal-length windows share every interior element.
feedback.details: Only the outgoing left value and incoming right value change the aggregate.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.add_both: The old left value leaves the interval.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.subtract_both: The new right value enters the interval.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_difference: That is circular and does not define the new sum.

items.prompt: Using P[0] = 0 and P[i + 1] = P[i] + a[i], which formula gives the inclusive range sum a[l] + ... + a[r]?

options.text: P[r] - P[l].

feedback.reason: The prefix through r contains the desired range plus everything before l.
feedback.details: Subtracting P[l] removes exactly the elements before the interval.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.add_prefixes: The shared prefix must be removed, not added.

items.prompt: Values arrive as a stream, and after each arrival the system must output the sum of the most recent k values. Which state is appropriate?

options.text: Maintain a rolling sum and enough state to remove the value leaving the last-k window.
Wait until the entire stream ends, then build a prefix array.
Sort all values seen so far.
Rescan all prior values after every arrival.

feedback.reason: The result is tied to one evolving interval at the stream frontier.
feedback.details: A rolling sum plus a queue or circular buffer for the outgoing value supports online updates.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.recompute_history: Only the last k values matter.

items.prompt: A static array must answer sums for intervals requested in arbitrary order, including repeated and overlapping ranges. Which distinction is decisive?

options.text: Prefix state makes every query independent of the previously answered interval.
A sliding window is automatically O(1) for any jump from the previous query.
The word contiguous proves sliding window.
A solution with one loop is always preferable.

feedback.reason: Prefix sums encode every boundary relative to a common origin.
feedback.details: That supports random access, whereas a moving window is centered on one current interval.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.moving_previous: Moving a window across a large jump can require many updates.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.contiguous_keyword: Both methods reason about contiguous ranges.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_loop: Loop count does not define reusable state.

items.prompt: A valid prefix array for the current immutable data already exists. A new range-sum query arrives. What should the algorithm do?

options.text: Use the stored prefix difference directly.
Scan from the previous query's interval to the new one.
Recompute all prefix sums for every query.
Sort the queried interval first.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing should be credited when it is already available and valid.
feedback.details: The query cost is the boundary subtraction, not another full construction.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.build_window: Existing prefix state already answers the query in constant time.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rebuild_prefix: The data is unchanged, so rebuilding wastes O(n) work.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_query: The sum does not require reordering.

items.prompt: Both a rolling sum and prefix sums can compute every length-k block sum in O(n) total time. Which additional fact often favors the rolling sum?

options.text: It can use O(1) aggregate state instead of an O(n) prefix array.
It answers arbitrary later intervals without retaining data.
It makes all future array updates free.
It can ignore original positions.

feedback.reason: When the workload is one sequential enumeration of equal-length intervals, a full prefix table may store more state than needed.
feedback.details: Both are correct; the space and reuse requirements decide between them.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.handles_random_queries: A single rolling state only represents the current block.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.works_after_updates_free: Updates still change later block values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.removes_contiguity: The method depends on contiguous movement.

items.prompt: A variable window maintains the invariant "current interval is valid" while the right boundary advances. What distinguishes this from a prefix array?

options.text: The window state drives boundary decisions for one evolving candidate interval.
A prefix array itself decides which left boundary to remove.
They are identical because both store sums.
Prefix sums cannot represent contiguous intervals.

feedback.reason: A sliding window couples an aggregate to a boundary-update rule.
feedback.details: Prefix sums answer what an interval contains once its boundaries are known; they do not automatically choose those boundaries.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_moves: Prefix values provide interval arithmetic but not the window's validity policy.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_structure: They organize state for different access patterns.

items.prompt: A task needs the sum and count of flagged elements for many static interval queries. Which extension is natural?

options.text: Build one prefix sequence for sums and another for flag counts.
Maintain one current interval and assume all queries share it.
Sort flagged values away from their positions.
Scan every interval twice.

feedback.reason: Additive interval features can each use cumulative state.
feedback.details: The query subtracts matching prefixes for every requested metric.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_current_window: Arbitrary queries can have unrelated boundaries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.recompute_metrics: Both additive metrics can be precomputed.

items.prompt: Which workload properties justify retaining only one evolving interval? Select all that apply.

options.text: Candidate intervals are visited in adjacent boundary order.
The aggregate can be updated from outgoing and incoming elements.
The answer is needed as a stream frontier advances.
Queries jump among arbitrary static intervals.

feedback.reason: Moving windows are strongest when one evolving interval captures the workload.
feedback.details: Random interval access is a different state requirement.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.local_update: This avoids recomputing shared interior work.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.online_output: A moving state supports online emission.

items.prompt: Which workload properties justify cumulative state at every boundary? Select all that apply.

options.text: Many interval queries over unchanged data.
Queries arrive in arbitrary boundary order.
The interval value is a difference of cumulative additive state.
Only the current last-k stream interval is ever requested.

feedback.reason: Prefix state is a reusable static index for additive interval information.
feedback.details: A single frontier-local interval does not need the whole index.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_frontier: A rolling window is more direct.

items.prompt: Which tasks can be solved correctly by either a moving sum or prefix sums? Select all that apply.

options.text: Compute all length-k block sums in one static array.
Compute the sum of one known interval.
Find the maximum length-k block sum.
Emit the last-k sum before future values are known using only offline prefix construction.

feedback.reason: Correctness alone may leave both state models available.
feedback.details: Online timing and memory determine whether full precomputation is legal or useful.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.online_last_k: Offline prefix construction cannot satisfy the online timing contract.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_fixed_blocks: Both can achieve O(n) total time.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.one_fixed_block: A direct scan, prefix difference, or prepared moving pass can all be correct.

items.prompt: Which observations are insufficient by themselves to choose sliding window? Select all that apply.

options.text: The prompt mentions a contiguous subarray.
The objective contains the word sum.
The implementation might use left and right indices.
Each next candidate removes one value and adds one value.

feedback.reason: Surface wording does not identify the required state model.
feedback.details: A legal incremental transition between adjacent candidates is real evidence.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.adjacent_update: This is a substantive moving-state signal.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.contiguous_word: Prefix sums also operate on contiguous ranges.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.two_indices: Variable names do not establish a discard rule.

elements.text: Use it to initialize the requested result.
For each next position, subtract the outgoing value and add the incoming value.
Evaluate the new interval and update the result.

feedback.reason: The first interval establishes state; every later interval reuses its overlap.
feedback.details: Updating before evaluating ensures the aggregate matches the new boundaries.

elements.text: Create a prefix sequence with a zero sentinel.
Accumulate P[i + 1] = P[i] + a[i] for the static data.

feedback.reason: Prefix preprocessing is independent of any one later query.
feedback.details: The sentinel convention makes inclusive interval formulas uniform.

items.prompt: Arrange the decision process between moving and precomputed state.

elements.text: State whether intervals are sequential candidates or arbitrary queries.
State whether answers are online or may wait for preprocessing.
Determine whether cumulative state will serve many future requests.
Compare legal time, auxiliary space, and update invalidation.

feedback.reason: The distinction follows access pattern and state lifetime.
feedback.details: The word contiguous does not replace this workload analysis.

items.prompt: Compute all length-k sums in a static array of n values. Compare a rolling-sum scan with building an n-sized prefix array and querying all n - k + 1 windows.
constraints: Output is streamed and excluded from auxiliary space.

feedback.reason: Both strategies are linear for this workload.
feedback.details: The moving sum uses less retained state, while the prefix array remains reusable for other interval queries.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Use a moving window when one sequential traversal can maintain exactly the state for the current contiguous interval; use prefix state when arbitrary interval values must be reconstructed from precomputed cumulative information.
